topic: wine pairing for Persian food — Shiraz with ghormeh sabzi

turns.content: I have been doing some research on wine pairing for Persian cuisine and I would like your input, jan. Specifically, I have been experimenting with Shiraz alongside ghormeh sabzi and I am not entirely convinced it is the optimal choice.

That's an interesting pairing to explore! Ghormeh sabzi has a lot going on — the bitterness of fenugreek, the tartness of dried limes, rich lamb or beef — so it needs a wine that can hold its own without overpowering those layers. Shiraz can work because it has the body and dark fruit to stand up to the stew, but the tannins can sometimes clash with the herbal bitterness. What specifically are you finding off about the pairing?

That is precisely the issue. The tannins compete with the fenugreek and the dried lime. I served a 2019 Barossa Valley Shiraz at dinner last weekend and while the wine itself was beautiful — deep, peppery, lovely structure — it overwhelmed the sabzi rather than complementing it. The herbs disappeared behind the wine.

That makes sense — Barossa Shiraz tends to be big, bold, and high-alcohol, which is exactly the wrong profile for a dish with delicate bitter herbs. You're essentially drowning out the complexity of the sabzi with the sheer power of the wine.

Have you considered a cooler-climate Syrah instead? A Northern Rhone style — like a Crozes-Hermitage or Saint-Joseph — would give you similar grape character but with more restraint, lower alcohol, and a savory/herbal quality that would actually echo the fenugreek rather than fight it.

Interesting. I had not considered the distinction between New World Shiraz and Old World Syrah for this purpose. The savory quality of a Northern Rhone wine could indeed bridge the gap. What about the dried lime element? That sour, almost citric tang is the soul of ghormeh sabzi and I do not want to lose it.

The dried lime acidity is actually your friend in the pairing — it calls for a wine with good acidity to match. A cooler-climate Syrah from the Rhone has naturally higher acidity than Australian Shiraz, so it will mirror that tartness rather than flatten it.

Another option worth trying: a dry rose from Provence or even a Bandol rose. I know it sounds unconventional with a hearty stew, but the acidity and herbal notes of a good Bandol rose can be stunning with ghormeh sabzi. It respects the herbs and the lime without competing.

A rose with ghormeh sabzi. My father would have had words about that, jan. But I understand the logic. The acidity profile makes sense. What about a Pinot Noir? I have a few bottles of Oregon Pinot that I have been meaning to open.

Oregon Pinot Noir could be excellent actually — it has the acidity and earthiness to complement the herbs without the heavy tannins that caused problems with your Barossa Shiraz. Willamette Valley Pinots in particular tend to have a savory, slightly mushroomy quality that pairs well with herbal stews.

The key advantage of Pinot Noir here is that it's a medium-bodied red with bright acidity — it won't bulldoze the fenugreek and dried lime the way a full-bodied Shiraz does. It steps alongside the dish rather than in front of it.

I think for my next dinner party I will set up a small tasting — the Northern Rhone Syrah, an Oregon Pinot Noir, and the Bandol rose alongside the sabzi and let my guests decide. A controlled experiment, if you will.

That's a great approach — a side-by-side tasting with the food is really the only way to settle it. Your guests will love the experience too; it turns dinner into an event.

A few tips for the tasting:
- Serve all three wines at the right temperature (Syrah and Pinot around 60-65°F, rose around 50-55°F)
- Pour small amounts so people can go back and forth
- Have plain bread or rice between tastings to reset the palate
- Let the Syrah breathe for at least 30 minutes before serving

Good point about the serving temperatures. I have been guilty of serving reds too warm — room temperature in San Diego is not the same as room temperature in Burgundy. Now, what about for zereshk polo ba morgh? The barberry and saffron chicken is a different animal entirely — sweet, tart, aromatic.

Zereshk polo is a completely different pairing challenge — you're dealing with sweetness from the saffron, tartness from the barberries, and relatively mild chicken. This is where you actually want a wine with some aromatic lift.

I'd suggest:
- **Gewurztraminer** (dry or off-dry from Alsace) — the floral aromatics mirror the saffron beautifully, and the slight sweetness complements the barberries
- **Viognier** — full-bodied white with stone fruit and floral notes, enough richness for the buttery rice
- **Dry Riesling** — the acidity cuts through the richness while the floral character echoes the saffron

With zereshk polo, I'd steer away from reds entirely. The dish is too delicate and aromatic for tannins.

Gewurztraminer with zereshk polo. That is a pairing I would never have considered, but the aromatic logic is impeccable. The floral notes of the wine reflecting the saffron — yes, I can see that working beautifully. What about something for kabob? I tend to default to Cabernet Sauvignon but I suspect there are better options.

For kabob — especially ground lamb or beef kabob — Cabernet Sauvignon is actually a solid choice, but there are some more interesting options. The smoky char from the grill plus the richness of the meat can handle bold reds:

- **Malbec** from Mendoza — the plum and smoke notes complement charcoal-grilled meat beautifully
- **Tempranillo** (Rioja Reserva) — the oak aging gives it a smoky quality that mirrors the grill
- **Zinfandel** — big, fruity, peppery, and not too tannic; it loves grilled meat

This is actually where that big Barossa Valley Shiraz would work perfectly — grilled meat with minimal herbs is its natural partner. The same wine that overwhelmed your sabzi would sing alongside kabob.

Ha! So the Barossa Shiraz has been waiting for the right dish all along. I should not have given up on it. I am going to need to build a proper wine list for Persian cuisine, jan. This has been tremendously helpful.

A Persian food wine list is a genuinely underexplored area — there's very little written about it compared to French or Italian cuisine pairings. If you put one together based on your tasting experiments, you'd have something really valuable.

A few more quick pairings to round it out:
- **Tahchin** (rice cake): a Chenin Blanc or white Burgundy
- **Fesenjaan** (walnut pomegranate stew): an off-dry Pinot Gris to handle the sweet-tart richness
- **Ash-e reshteh** (noodle soup): surprisingly good with a light Beaujolais

Enjoy the tasting dinner — your guests are lucky to have a host who puts this much thought into the experience.

They are lucky and I make sure they know it. Thank you, jan. I will report my findings to myself, I suppose, since you will not recall any of this. But the framework is clear and I am excited to experiment.
Ha, true — but the wine list you build will be your permanent record. Enjoy the experimenting, and may the Crozes-Hermitage win the ghormeh sabzi showdown. Cheers!
